quick_facts.label: What it is
quick_facts.value: A national eligibility examination in Science & Technology conducted by the National Testing Agency (NTA) on behalf of CSIR, for Junior Research Fellowship (JRF), Assistant Professor eligibility, and/or Ph.D. admission in Indian universities and colleges.

quick_facts.label: June 2026 exam subjects
quick_facts.value: The June 2026 cycle covered Chemical Sciences; Earth, Atmospheric, Ocean and Planetary Sciences; Life Sciences; Mathematical Sciences; and Physical Sciences.

quick_facts.label: Exam mode and duration
quick_facts.value: For the June 2026 cycle, the examination was a three-hour, objective-type multiple-choice Computer Based Test (CBT).

quick_facts.label: Primary postgraduate eligibility
quick_facts.value: For June 2026, General/Unreserved/General-EWS candidates needed at least 55% in a recognised master's degree or equivalent; OBC-NCL, SC, ST, PwD/PwBD and third-gender candidates needed at least 50%. Final-year/result-awaited master's candidates could apply provisionally, subject to later completion requirements.

quick_facts.label: Four-year bachelor's route
quick_facts.value: For June 2026, candidates with a four-year/eight-semester bachelor's degree required at least 75% aggregate marks (with a stated 5-percentage-point relaxation for specified categories). Qualification through this route could lead to JRF and Ph.D. admission, but not Assistant Professor appointment eligibility.

quick_facts.label: Age rules in June 2026
quick_facts.value: The JRF upper age limit was 30 years as of July 2026, with specified relaxations. There was no upper age limit for Assistant Professor eligibility or Ph.D. admission.

quick_facts.label: June 2026 application fee context
quick_facts.value: The June 2026 bulletin listed application fees of ₹1,150 for General, ₹600 for General-EWS/OBC-NCL, and ₹325 for SC/ST/PwD/PwBD/third-gender applicants, plus applicable payment-gateway charges. Fees can change by cycle.

sections.heading: Purpose and what qualifying means
paragraphs: CSIR-UGC NET is an eligibility examination rather than a degree programme or a direct recruitment examination. In the June 2026 framework, NTA conducted it on CSIR’s behalf for three result categories: (1) JRF plus Assistant Professor eligibility plus Ph.D. admission, (2) Assistant Professor eligibility plus Ph.D. admission, and (3) Ph.D. admission only.

A Category-1 JRF qualification supports research training under eligible supervisors and institutions. The bulletin states that the fellowships are accepted in universities, IITs, postgraduate colleges, government research establishments—including CSIR establishments—and recognised public- or private-sector industrial R&D organisations in India. Qualification does not itself guarantee employment.

For Category-2 and Category-3 candidates using NET for Ph.D. admission, the June 2026 bulletin states that the NET result is valid for one year for that purpose; the concerned university or higher-education institution uses a 70% written-test and 30% interview/viva-voce weighting. JRF-qualified candidates enter Ph.D. programmes through an interview under the applicable UGC Ph.D. regulations.
bullets: A qualifying certificate for Assistant Professor eligibility has no stated end-period of validity in the June 2026 bulletin.

A NET qualification may strengthen routes into doctoral research, academic teaching eligibility, and research roles, but an employer, laboratory, or university retains its own selection and appointment processes.

sections.heading: Eligibility: degree, marks, age, and nationality
paragraphs: For the June 2026 cycle, the examination was for bona fide Indian citizens. The usual route was a recognised master's degree or equivalent with at least 55% marks for General/Unreserved/General-EWS candidates, or 50% for OBC-NCL, SC, ST, PwD/PwBD and third-gender candidates. Candidates pursuing the qualifying master's degree, awaiting final results, or delayed by their qualifying examination could apply provisionally, subject to the bulletin’s completion deadlines and minimum marks.

A four-year/eight-semester bachelor's degree route was also available. Applicants needed at least 75% aggregate marks or the equivalent grade, with a 5% relaxation stated for SC, ST, OBC-NCL, differently abled, EWS and other categories as decided by the Commission. Candidates qualifying on this bachelor's-degree basis could receive JRF and pursue Ph.D. admission, but were not eligible for Assistant Professor appointment through that route.

For JRF in June 2026, applicants had to be no more than 30 years old as of July 2026. The bulletin provided up to five years’ relaxation for women and OBC-NCL, SC, ST, PwD/PwBD and third-gender candidates, as well as specified provisions for research experience and armed-forces service. It set no upper age limit for the Assistant Professor or Ph.D.-admission routes. Applicants should always check the information bulletin for their own cycle because eligibility rules and dates may be updated.
bullets: A four-year bachelor's student in the final semester/year could apply provisionally for JRF/Ph.D. routes, subject to completing the prescribed requirements after the result.
A foreign qualification should be assessed for equivalence through UGC; the bulletin also advises holders of Indian-institute postgraduate diplomas to establish master's equivalence where needed.

sections.heading: Test format and subjects
paragraphs: The June 2026 CSIR-UGC NET was delivered in CBT mode, in Hindi and English, with a three-hour duration and objective multiple-choice questions. It had three parts with no break between them. Part A is common across subjects and tests general aptitude; Part B covers conventional subject-related MCQs; and Part C asks higher-order analytical questions that require application of scientific concepts.

The five test papers were Chemical Sciences (701), Earth, Atmospheric, Ocean and Planetary Sciences (702), Life Sciences (703), Mathematical Sciences (704), and Physical Sciences (705). Each paper had a maximum score of 200, but question counts, permitted attempts, marks per question, and negative-marking rules differed by subject. Candidates should use the scheme for their own subject rather than assuming a common attempt limit.
CSIR HRDG publishes the subject syllabi and model question papers. These official materials are the appropriate base for mapping a preparation plan to Parts A, B, and C.
bullets: Part A: logical reasoning, graphical analysis, analytical and numerical ability, quantitative comparison, series formation, and puzzles.
Part C: analytical, application-oriented scientific questions.
Negative marking applies according to the subject-specific scheme; for example, Mathematical Sciences had no negative marking in Part C in the June 2026 bulletin.

sections.heading: Application process and June 2026 cycle status
paragraphs: NTA’s June 2026 process was online-only through the Joint CSIR-UGC NET portal. The process required candidates to register using their own email address and mobile number, complete the application, upload the required photograph and signature files, pay the fee, and retain the confirmation page. The bulletin also required candidates to select four examination-city preferences.

For the June 2026 cycle, online applications were open from May 27 through June 19, 2026, fee payment closed on June 20, and the correction window ran June 22–23. The examination was scheduled for July 17–18, 2026. These are historical, cycle-specific dates and not a timetable for a future cycle; candidates should monitor the official NTA portal and bulletin for the next application notice.

For this cycle, the NTA issued the city-intimation notice on July 8 and the admit-card notice on July 13. The city-intimation slip was not an admit card; candidates needed to download the separate admit card using their application number and password.
bullets: Use a personal email address and mobile number; the bulletin says these cannot later be changed.
Keep the application number, confirmation page, admit card, and score card for future reference.
Check the correction-window rules carefully: some information can only be corrected during that limited period.

sections.heading: Preparation approach using official materials
paragraphs: Start by selecting the applicable subject paper and downloading its official CSIR HRDG syllabus. Turn each syllabus unit into a checklist, then distinguish between factual/revision-heavy Part B topics and the concept-application work expected in Part C. Maintain a separate aptitude practice track for the common Part A areas listed in the information bulletin.

Use CSIR HRDG’s official model papers to become familiar with the three-part structure, subject-specific attempt caps, and marking rules. In timed practice, stop attempting questions once the subject-specific permitted number has been reached; the bulletin notes that, where candidates answer more than the permitted number, only the first permitted responses are evaluated.

Review each mock or past-question attempt by error type: missing subject knowledge, a faulty calculation, misreading, excessive time spent, or an avoidable negative-marking risk. This creates a targeted revision plan while keeping practice aligned to the official syllabus and format.
bullets: Build conceptual fluency for Part C rather than relying solely on recall.
Practice Part A regularly because it is common to all five science papers.
Before the exam, re-check the current-cycle bulletin because question counts, permitted attempts, and negative marking are subject-specific and may be revised.

sections.heading: Outcomes: Ph.D., teaching eligibility, and research pathways
paragraphs: The result category matters. Category 1 confers eligibility for JRF, Assistant Professor appointment, and Ph.D. admission; Category 2 is for Assistant Professor appointment and Ph.D. admission but not JRF; Category 3 is for Ph.D. admission only. A score alone is not an appointment letter or Ph.D. offer, so candidates must still meet the recruiting institution’s or university’s programme-specific requirements.

As dated June 2026 fellowship context, a JRF selected through CSIR-NET was listed at ₹37,000 per month for the first two years, plus a ₹20,000 annual contingent grant reimbursed directly to the fellow. After two years, a fellow registered for a Ph.D. could be upgraded to SRF at ₹42,000 per month following assessment of research progress. These terms, fellowship numbers, and amounts are policy-dependent and can change.

The fellowship can be held at eligible academic, government-research, and recognised industrial R&D settings in India. This supports the node’s links to doctoral study and research-oriented careers, while actual research-scientist and government-scientist jobs require separate recruitment processes and eligibility criteria.
bullets: Assistant Professor certificate validity: no end-period is stated in the June 2026 bulletin.
Category-2 and Category-3 NET marks were valid for Ph.D. admission for one year from result declaration in the June 2026 framework.
The bulletin explicitly states that a JRF award is for a fixed tenure and does not guarantee subsequent employment.
